sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Pray you, tell him
I am his fortune’s vassal, and I send him
The greatness he has got. I hourly learn
A doctrine of obedience, and would gladly
Look him i’ th’ face.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Pray you, tell him
I am his fortune’s vassal, and I send him
The greatness he has got. I hourly learn
A doctrine of obedience, and would gladly
Look him i’ th’ face.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
This I’ll report, dear lady.
Have comfort, for I know your plight is pitied
Of him that caused it.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
This I’ll report, dear lady.
Have comfort, for I know your plight is pitied

GALLUS and Roman soldiers enter from behind and take CLEOPATRA prisoner

sentences.speaker: GALLUS
sentences.text:
You see how easily she may be surprised.
[To the soldiers] Guard her till Caesar come.

sentences.speaker: GALLUS
sentences.text:
You see how easily she may be surprised.
[To the soldiers] Guard her till Caesar come.

sentences.speaker: IRAS
sentences.text: Royal Queen!

sentences.speaker: CHARMIAN
sentences.text: O Cleopatra! Thou art taken, Queen.

sentences.speaker: CHARMIAN
sentences.text: O Cleopatra! Thou art taken, Queen.

sentences.speaker: CHARMIAN
sentences.text: O Cleopatra! Thou art taken, Queen.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Quick, quick, good hands.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Quick, quick, good hands.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
Hold, worthy lady, hold!
Do not yourself such wrong, who are in this
Relieved but not betrayed.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
Hold, worthy lady, hold!
Do not yourself such wrong, who are in this
Relieved but not betrayed.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
Hold, worthy lady, hold!
Do not yourself such wrong, who are in this
Relieved but not betrayed.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
What, of death too,
That rids our dogs of languish?

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
What, of death too,
That rids our dogs of languish?

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
Cleopatra,
Do not abuse my master’s bounty by
Th’ undoing of yourself. Let the world see
His nobleness well acted, which your death
Will never let come forth.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
Cleopatra,
Do not abuse my master’s bounty by

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Where art thou, Death?
Come hither, come! Come, come and take a queen
Worth many babes and beggars!

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Where art thou, Death?
Come hither, come! Come, come and take a queen
Worth many babes and beggars!

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Where art thou, Death?
Come hither, come! Come, come and take a queen
Worth many babes and beggars!

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text: Oh, temperance, lady!

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Sir, I will eat no meat, I’ll not drink, sir.
If idle talk will once be necessary,
I’ll not sleep neither. This mortal house I’ll ruin,
Do Caesar what he can. Know, sir, that I
Will not wait pinioned at your master’s court,
Nor once be chastised with the sober eye
Of dull Octavia. Shall they hoist me up
And show me to the shouting varletry
Of censuring Rome? Rather a ditch in Egypt
Be gentle grave unto me. Rather on Nilus’ mud
Lay me stark naked and let the waterflies
Blow me into abhorring. Rather make
My country’s high pyramides my gibbet
And hang me up in chains!

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
You do extend
These thoughts of horror further than you shall
Find cause in Caesar.
Enter DOLABELLA

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
Proculeius,
What thou hast done thy master Caesar knows,
And he hath sent for thee. For the Queen,
I’ll take her to my guard.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
Proculeius,
What thou hast done thy master Caesar knows,
And he hath sent for thee. For the Queen,
I’ll take her to my guard.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
So, Dolabella,
It shall content me best. Be gentle to her.
[To CLEOPATRA] To Caesar I will speak what you shall
please,
If you’ll employ me to him.

sentences.speaker: PROCULEIUS
sentences.text:
So, Dolabella,
It shall content me best. Be gentle to her.
[To CLEOPATRA] To Caesar I will speak what you shall
please,
If you’ll employ me to him.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Say I would die.
Exit PROCULEIUS

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Most noble Empress, you have heard of me?

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: I cannot tell.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Assuredly you know me.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
No matter, sir, what I have heard or known.
You laugh when boys or women tell their dreams.
Is ’t not your trick?

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
No matter, sir, what I have heard or known.
You laugh when boys or women tell their dreams.
Is ’t not your trick?

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
No matter, sir, what I have heard or known.
You laugh when boys or women tell their dreams.
Is ’t not your trick?

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: I understand not, madam.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
I dreamt there was an emperor Antony.
Oh, such another sleep, that I might see
But such another man!

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
I dreamt there was an emperor Antony.
Oh, such another sleep, that I might see
But such another man!

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: If it might please ye—

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
His face was as the heavens, and therein stuck
A sun and moon, which kept their course and lighted
The little “O,” the earth.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
His face was as the heavens, and therein stuck
A sun and moon, which kept their course and lighted
The little “O,” the earth.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Most sovereign creature—

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
His legs bestrid the ocean. His reared arm
Crested the world. His voice was propertied
As all the tunèd spheres, and that to friends.
But when he meant to quail and shake the orb,
He was as rattling thunder. For his bounty,
There was no winter in ’t, an autumn ’twas
That grew the more by reaping. His delights
Were dolphinlike; they showed his back above
The element they lived in. In his livery
Walked crowns and crownets. Realms and islands were
As plates dropped from his pocket.

He was as rattling thunder. For his bounty,
There was no winter in ’t, an autumn ’twas
That grew the more by reaping. His delights
Were dolphinlike; they showed his back above
The element they lived in. In his livery
Walked crowns and crownets. Realms and islands were
As plates dropped from his pocket.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Cleopatra—

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Think you there was or might be such a man
As this I dreamt of?

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Gentle madam, no.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
You lie up to the hearing of the gods.
But if there be nor ever were one such,
It’s past the size of dreaming. Nature wants stuff
To vie strange forms with fancy, yet t’ imagine
An Antony were nature’s piece ’gainst fancy,
Condemning shadows quite.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
Hear me, good madam.
Your loss is as yourself, great, and you bear it
As answering to the weight. Would I might never
O’ertake pursued success, but I do feel,
By the rebound of yours, a grief that smites
My very heart at root.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
I thank you, sir.
Know you what Caesar means to do with me?

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
I thank you, sir.
Know you what Caesar means to do with me?

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: I am loath to tell you what I would you knew.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Nay, pray you, sir.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Though he be honorable—

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: He’ll lead me, then, in triumph.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Madam, he will. I know ’t.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Madam, he will. I know ’t.
Flourish. Enter CAESAR, PROCULEIUS, GALLUS, MAECENAS, and other ATTENDANTS

sentences.speaker: ATTENDANTS
sentences.text: Make way there! Caesar!

sentences.speaker: ATTENDANTS
sentences.text: Make way there! Caesar!

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text: Which is the Queen of Egypt?

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: [To CLEOPATRA] It is the Emperor, madam.
CLEOPATRA kneels

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Arise, you shall not kneel.
I pray you, rise. Rise, Egypt.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Arise, you shall not kneel.
I pray you, rise. Rise, Egypt.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Arise, you shall not kneel.
I pray you, rise. Rise, Egypt.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Sir, the gods
Will have it thus. My master and my lord
I must obey.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Sir, the gods
Will have it thus. My master and my lord
I must obey.
CLEOPATRA stands

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Take to you no hard thoughts.
The record of what injuries you did us,
Though written in our flesh, we shall remember
As things but done by chance.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Take to you no hard thoughts.
The record of what injuries you did us,
Though written in our flesh, we shall remember
As things but done by chance.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Sole sir o’ th’ world,
I cannot project mine own cause so well
To make it clear, but do confess I have
Been laden with like frailties which before
Have often shamed our sex.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Sole sir o’ th’ world,
I cannot project mine own cause so well
To make it clear, but do confess I have
Been laden with like frailties which before
Have often shamed our sex.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Cleopatra, know
We will extenuate rather than enforce.
If you apply yourself to our intents,
Which towards you are most gentle, you shall find
A benefit in this change, but if you seek
To lay on me a cruelty by taking
Antony’s course, you shall bereave yourself
Of my good purposes and put your children
To that destruction which I’ll guard them from
If thereon you rely. I’ll take my leave.

And may, through all the world! ’Tis yours, and we,
Your scutcheons and your signs of conquest, shall
Hang in what place you please. Here, my good lord.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text: You shall advise me in all for Cleopatra.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
This is the brief of money, plate, and jewels
I am possessed of. ’Tis exactly valued,
Not petty things admitted. Where’s Seleucus?

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:

I am possessed of. ’Tis exactly valued,
Not petty things admitted. Where’s Seleucus?

Enter SELEUCUS

sentences.speaker: SELEUCUS
sentences.text: Here, madam.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
This is my treasurer. Let him speak, my lord,
Upon his peril, that I have reserved
To myself nothing. — Speak the truth, Seleucus.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
This is my treasurer. Let him speak, my lord,
Upon his peril, that I have reserved
To myself nothing. — Speak the truth, Seleucus.

sentences.speaker: SELEUCUS
sentences.text:
Madam, I had rather seal my lips
Than to my peril speak that which is not.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: What have I kept back?

sentences.speaker: SELEUCUS
sentences.text: Enough to purchase what you have made known.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Nay, blush not, Cleopatra. I approve
Your wisdom in the deed.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Nay, blush not, Cleopatra. I approve
Your wisdom in the deed.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
See, Caesar! Oh, behold
How pomp is followed! Mine will now be yours,
And, should we shift estates, yours would be mine.
The ingratitude of this Seleucus does
Even make me wild. [To SELEUCUS] O slave, of no more
trust
Than love that’s hired!
What, goest thou back? Thou shalt
Go back, I warrant thee! But I’ll catch thine eyes,
Though they had wings. Slave, soulless villain, dog!
Oh, rarely base!

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text: Good Queen, let us entreat you—

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
O Caesar, what a wounding shame is this,
That thou, vouchsafing here to visit me,
Doing the honor of thy lordliness
To one so meek, that mine own servant should
Parcel the sum of my disgraces by
Addition of his envy! Say, good Caesar,
That I some lady trifles have reserved,
Immoment toys, things of such dignity
As we greet modern friends withal, and say
Some nobler token I have kept apart
For Livia and Octavia, to induce
Their mediation, must I be unfolded
With one that I have bred? The gods! It smites me
Beneath the fall I have. [To SELEUCUS] Prithee, go
hence,
Or I shall show the cinders of my spirit
Through th’ ashes of my chance. Wert thou a man,
Thou wouldst have mercy on me.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text: Forbear, Seleucus.
Exit SELEUCUS

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Be it known that we, the greatest, are misthought
For things that others do, and when we fall
We answer others’ merits in our name,
Are therefore to be pitied.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Be it known that we, the greatest, are misthought
For things that others do, and when we fall
We answer others’ merits in our name,

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Cleopatra,
Not what you have reserved nor what acknowledged
Put we i’ th’ roll of conquest. Still be ’t yours.
Bestow it at your pleasure, and believe
Caesar’s no merchant, to make prize with you
Of things that merchants sold. Therefore be cheered.
Make not your thoughts your prison. No, dear Queen,
For we intend so to dispose you as
Yourself shall give us counsel. Feed and sleep.
Our care and pity is so much upon you
That we remain your friend. And so, adieu.

Finish, good lady. The bright day is done,

[To CHARMIAN] Hie thee again.
I have spoke already, and it is provided.
Go put it to the haste.

sentences.text:
[To CHARMIAN] Hie thee again.
I have spoke already, and it is provided.
Go put it to the haste.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
[To CHARMIAN] Hie thee again.
I have spoke already, and it is provided.
Go put it to the haste.

sentences.speaker: CHARMIAN
sentences.text: Madam, I will.
Enter DOLABELLA

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: Where’s the Queen?

sentences.speaker: CHARMIAN
sentences.text: Behold, sir.
Exit

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Dolabella!

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
Madam, as thereto sworn by your command,
Which my love makes religion to obey,
I tell you this: Caesar through Syria
Intends his journey, and within three days
You with your children will he send before.
Make your best use of this. I have performed
Your pleasure and my promise.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Dolabella,
I shall remain your debtor.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
I your servant.
Adieu, good Queen. I must attend on Caesar.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
I your servant.
Adieu, good Queen. I must attend on Caesar.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA

I your servant.
Adieu, good Queen. I must attend on Caesar.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Farewell, and thanks.
Exit DOLABELLA

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Now, Iras, what think’st thou?
Thou an Egyptian puppet shalt be shown
In Rome, as well as I. Mechanic slaves
With greasy aprons, rules, and hammers shall
Uplift us to the view. In their thick breaths,
Rank of gross diet, shall be enclouded,
And forced to drink their vapor.

sentences.speaker: IRAS
sentences.text: The gods forbid!

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Nay, ’tis most certain, Iras. Saucy lictors
Will catch at us like strumpets, and scald rhymers
Ballad us out o’ tune. The quick comedians
Extemporally will stage us and present
Our Alexandrian revels. Antony
Shall be brought drunken forth, and I shall see
Some squeaking Cleopatra boy my greatness
I’ th’ posture of a whore.

sentences.speaker: IRAS
sentences.text: Oh, the good gods!

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Nay, that’s certain.

sentences.speaker: IRAS
sentences.text:
I’ll never see ’t! For I am sure mine nails
Are stronger than mine eyes.

sentences.speaker: IRAS
sentences.text:
I’ll never see ’t! For I am sure mine nails
Are stronger than mine eyes.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Why, that’s the way
To fool their preparation and to conquer
Their most absurd intents.
Enter CHARMIAN

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Now, Charmian!
Show me, my women, like a queen. Go fetch
My best attires. I am again for Cydnus,
To meet Mark Antony. —Sirrah Iras, go.—
Now, noble Charmian, we’ll dispatch indeed,
And when thou hast done this chare I’ll give thee leave
To play till doomsday. [To IRAS] Bring our crown and
all.

Exit IRAS. A noise within

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Wherefore’s this noise?
Enter a GUARDSMAN

sentences.speaker: GUARDSMAN
sentences.text:
Here is a rural fellow
That will not be denied your Highness’ presence.
He brings you figs.

sentences.speaker: GUARDSMAN
sentences.text:
Here is a rural fellow
That will not be denied your Highness’ presence.
He brings you figs.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Let him come in.
Exit GUARDSMAN

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
What poor an instrument
May do a noble deed! He brings me liberty.
My resolution’s placed, and I have nothing
Of woman in me. Now from head to foot
I am marble-constant. Now the fleeting moon
No planet is of mine.

Enter GUARDSMAN, and COUNTRYMAN bringing in a basket

sentences.speaker: GUARDSMAN
sentences.text: This is the man.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Avoid, and leave him.
Exit GUARDSMAN

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Hast thou the pretty worm of Nilus there,
That kills and pains not?

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
Truly, I have him, but I would not be the party that
should desire you to touch him, for his biting is
immortal. Those that do die of it do seldom or never
recover.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
Truly, I have him, but I would not be the party that
should desire you to touch him, for his biting is
immortal. Those that do die of it do seldom or never
recover.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Remember’st thou any that have died on ’t?

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
Very many, men and women too. I heard of one of them no
longer than yesterday—a very honest woman, but
something given to lie, as a woman should not do but in
the way of honesty —how she died of the biting of it,
what pain she felt. Truly, she makes a very good report
o’ th’ worm. But he that will believe all that they say
shall never be saved by half that they do. But this is
most falliable, the worm’s an odd worm.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Get thee hence, farewell.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text: I wish you all joy of the worm.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text: I wish you all joy of the worm.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Farewell.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
You must think this, look you, that the worm will do
his kind.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Ay, ay. Farewell.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Ay, ay. Farewell.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
Look you, the worm is not to be trusted but in the
keeping of wise people, for indeed there is no goodness
in the worm.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Take thou no care. It shall be heeded.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Take thou no care. It shall be heeded.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
Very good. Give it nothing, I pray you, for it is not
worth the feeding.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
Very good. Give it nothing, I pray you, for it is not
worth the feeding.

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text: Will it eat me?

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN
sentences.text:
You must not think I am so simple but I know the devil
himself will not eat a woman. I know that a woman is a
dish for the gods, if the devil dress her not. But,
truly, these same whoreson devils do the gods great harm
in their women, for in every ten that they make, the
devils mar five.

sentences.speaker: COUNTRYMAN

Enter IRAS with royal attire

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Give me my robe. Put on my crown. I have
Immortal longings in me. Now no more
The juice of Egypt’s grape shall moist this lip.

She kisses them. IRAS falls and dies
She kisses them. IRAS falls and dies

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Have I the aspic in my lips? Dost fall?
If thou and nature can so gently part,
The stroke of death is as a lover’s pinch,
Which hurts, and is desired. Dost thou lie still?
If thus thou vanishest, thou tell’st the world
It is not worth leave-taking.

Dissolve, thick cloud, and rain, that I may say
The gods themselves do weep!

If she first meet the curlèd Antony,
He’ll make demand of her and spend that kiss
Which is my heaven to have. —Come, thou mortal wretch,

sentences.speaker: CLEOPATRA
sentences.text:
Peace, peace!
Dost thou not see my baby at my breast,
That sucks the nurse asleep?

O Antony!—Nay, I will take thee too.

It is well done and fitting for a princess
Descended of so many royal kings.
Ah, soldier!

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text: How goes it here?

sentences.speaker: SECOND GUARD
sentences.text: All dead.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
Caesar, thy thoughts
Touch their effects in this. Thyself art coming
To see performed the dreaded act which thou
So sought’st to hinder.

Enter CAESAR and all his train, marching

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: A way there, a way for Caesar!

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
O sir, you are too sure an augurer.
That you did fear is done.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Bravest at the last,
She leveled at our purposes and, being royal,
Took her own way. The manner of their deaths?
I do not see them bleed.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR

sentences.speaker: FIRST GUARD
sentences.text:
Oh, Caesar,
This Charmian lived but now. She stood and spake.
I found her trimming up the diadem
On her dead mistress. Tremblingly she stood
And on the sudden dropped.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Oh, noble weakness!
If they had swallowed poison, ’twould appear
By external swelling, but she looks like sleep,
As she would catch another Antony
In her strong toil of grace.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA
sentences.text:
Here on her breast
There is a vent of blood, and something blown.
The like is on her arm.

sentences.speaker: DOLABELLA

sentences.speaker: FIRST GUARD
sentences.text:
This is an aspic’s trail, and these fig leaves
Have slime upon them, such as th’ aspic leaves
Upon the caves of Nile.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Most probable
That so she died, for her physician tells me
She hath pursued conclusions infinite
Of easy ways to die. Take up her bed
And bear her women from the monument.
She shall be buried by her Antony.
No grave upon the earth shall clip in it
A pair so famous. High events as these
Strike those that make them, and their story is
No less in pity than his glory which
Brought them to be lamented. Our army shall
In solemn show attend this funeral,
And then to Rome. Come, Dolabella, see
High order in this great solemnity.
